# **Wei Research Group**

## **[Purdue University](http://www.purdue.edu) - [Department of Chemistry](http://www.chem.purdue.edu)**

# Plasmonic nanomaterials

Surface plasmons are produced by the optical excitation of free electrons at metal-dielectric interfaces. Gold and silver nanostructures can be designed to support tunable plasmon modes, with diverse applications such as chemical sensing, biological imaging, photonics, and energy harvesting. Many of these applications rely on localized electromagnetic fields that are generated at plasmon resonance. The field factors produced by single metal nanoparticles are relatively modest, but can intensify dramatically when nanoparticles are close together, resulting in strongly coupled plasmons.

We have studied the collective plasmonic properties of gold nanoparticle 2D arrays ([formed by self-assembly](http://www.chem.purdue.edu/awei/publications/ja0160344.pdf "Self-assembly of Au nanoparticle arrays")) and gold nanorod 2D arrays ([prepared by templated synthesis](http://www.chem.purdue.edu/awei/publications/Wei34.pdf "Gold Nanorod Arrays grown in Polyethylenimine-coated Alumina Templates")), whose optical absorbance and reflectance peaks span the range of visible and near-infrared wavelengths. Periodic nanorod arrays support localized plasmon resonances as a function of height, interparticle separation, and their dielectric medium; the gaps between nanorods act as [resonant cavities that can support a harmonic series of plasmon modes](https://pubs.acs.org/doi/abs/10.1021/nn8006477 "Gold Nanorod Arrays as Plasmonic Cavity Resonators"). These tunable features enable the engineering of metamaterials with nonclassical optical properties; for example, nanorod arrays are capable of supporting [hyperbolic plasmon modes that can enhance spontaneous emission and lasing at non-resonant wavelengths](http://pubs.acs.org/doi/abs/10.1021/acsphotonics.7b00010 "Gold Nanorod Arrays as Hyperbolic Nanomaterials") (collaboration with [Shalaev group](http://engineering.purdue.edu/~shalaev/ "Shalaev research group")).

##### [Gold Nanorod Arrays](../images/research/plasmonics/plasmonics_nanorod arrays.jpg "Gold nanorod arrays") ***From left to right:*gold nanorod 2D array (side and top views); vis-NIR reflectance with resonant extinctions, due to harmonics generated within periodic nanorod array; nanorods as hyperbolic metamaterial supporting spontaneous emission (lasing).**

![Gold Nanorod Arrays](../images/research/plasmonics/plasmonics_nanorod arrays.jpg)

For plasmon-enhanced spectroscopy and chemical sensing modalities such as surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), highly ordered metal nanostructures are not required. More commonly, SERS-active substrates rely on "hot spots" that can be generated by irregular structures or aggregates.  Examples from our lab include [nanoporous gold films etched with sharp edges](http://pubs.acs.org/doi/abs/10.1021/la902087e "Dithiocarbamate-Coated SERS Substrates"), and [magnetic gold nanoclusters (MGNCs)](http://pubs.rsc.org/en/Content/ArticleLanding/2015/NR/C4NR07658C#!divAbstract "DMAP-coated magnetic gold nanoclusters") whose aggregation can be induced by a magnetic field that produce hot spots on demand. Controlled surface functionalization can endow metal nanostructures with supramolecular function, and can be performed in ways that direct molecular analytes toward SERS-active sites. Raman-active surface ligands are also useful for reporting the adsorption of metal ions or molecules which have no SERS signatures of their own.

##### [SERS-active plasmonic nanomaterials](../images/research/plasmonics/plasmonics_SERS.jpg "SERS active plasmonic nanomaterials") ***Left*, nanoporous gold with localized supramolecular receptors for metal ion sensing. *Right*, magnetic gold nanoclusters (MGNCs) coated with DMAP, for selective TBBPA detection.**

![SERS-active plasmonic nanomaterials](../images/research/plasmonics/plasmonics_SERS.jpg)

# The Science of Plasmonics

Plasmonic nanoparticles - including gold, silver and platinum particles - are discrete metallic particles that have unique optical properties due to their size and shape, and are increasingly being incorporated into commercial products and technologies. These technologies, which span fields ranging from photovoltaics to biological and chemical sensors, take advantage of the extraordinary efficiency of plasmonic nanoparticles at absorbing and scattering light. Additionally, unlike most dyes and pigments, plasmonic nanoparticles have a color that depends on their size and shape and can be tuned to optimize performance for individual applications without changing the chemical composition of the material: for example, the solutions below contain gold or silver nanoparticles with controlled size and shape that tunes their optical response, resulting in vibrantly colored dispersions.

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/MG_2984-Edit_large.jpg?v=1492712937)

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/MG_2984-Edit_large.jpg?v=1492712937)

This module describes the origin of the surface plasmon resonance in metal nanoparticles and introduces the resulting optical properties for this interesting class of materials.

## Plasmonic Nanoparticles

Plasmonic nanoparticles are extremely strong absorbers and scatters of light and are used in lateral flow diagnostics, surface enhanced spectroscopy, labeling, and color changing sensors. By changing nanoparticle size, shape, and composition, the optical response can be tuned from the ultraviolet through the visible to the near-infrared regions of the electromagnetic spectrum. By shifting the absorption and scattering, the color of nanoparticle dispersions and films can also be tuned: for example, solutions of spherical gold nanoparticles are ruby red in color due to the strong scattering and absorption in the green region of the spectrum, while solutions of silver nanoparticles are yellow due to the plasmon resonance in the blue region of the spectrum (red and green light is unaffected).

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/products-spheres_116d55c7-1178-4c28-847e-2a44b2b1293a_large.jpg?v=1493073128)

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/products-spheres_116d55c7-1178-4c28-847e-2a44b2b1293a_large.jpg?v=1493073128)

In addition to metal nanoparticles, there have been recent examples of metal oxide nanoparticles doped with other metal atoms that show strong and tunable plasmon resonances. By changing the particle size, dopant and dopant concentration, plasmon resonances in the near-infrared (NIR) and short-wavelength infrared (SWIR) have been fabricated.

## What is a Surface Plasmon Resonance?

The remarkable optical properties of plasmonic materials occurs because the conduction electrons on the nanoparticle surface undergo a collective oscillation when excited by light at specific wavelengths (shown below). This oscillation, which is known as a ***surface plasmon resonance*** (SPR), results in the unusually strong scattering and absorption of light. When these resonances are excited, absorption and scattering intensities can be up to 40x higher than identically sized particles that are not plasmonic.

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/plasmon2_large.jpg?v=1492711626)

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/plasmon2_large.jpg?v=1492711626)

### The Physics of Plasmon Resonances

The basis for the effect is the plasmon resonance of the free electrons in the metal nanoparticle, which can be understood by studying the polarizability (the ease with which charges, such as the conduction electrons on the metal nanoparticle surface, undergo charge distribution and form partial dipoles). For a spherical nanoparticle, the quasi-static polarizability of the nanoparticle is given by

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/plasmonic-equation_large.png?v=1492711611)where *ε*1 is the wavelength dependent dielectric function of the nanoparticle and *ε*2 is the dielectric function of the medium which remains roughly constant regardless of wavelength. When the condition Re{*ε*1} = -2*ε*2 is satisfied, the particle is driven into resonance resulting in a strong increase in the absorption and/or scattering at that wavelength. The resonance condition depends on the wavelength dependent dielectric function of the nanoparticle as well as the dielectric function of the medium. Consequently, the nanoparticle optical properties are highly dependent on material composition, size, and the medium in which the particles are embedded. For example, increasing the aspect ratio of gold nanorods causes the plasmon resonance to shift from the visible into the NIR, as shown below.

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/plasmonic-equation_large.png?v=1492711611)

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/nanorods-full-spectrum_large.gif?v=1469683953)

![](//cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0257/8237/files/nanorods-full-spectrum_large.gif?v=1469683953)

Nanoparticle optical properties are also sensitive to the proximity of other plasmonic materials. When two or more plasmonic nanoparticles are near each other (with edge-to-edge separations of one particle diameter or less) their surface plasmons couple as the conduction electrons on each particle surface collectively oscillate. This effect is similar to molecular orbital theory in that plasmon coupling results in the oscillating electrons assuming the lowest energy state, causing the plasmon resonance wavelength of the coupled particles to red-shift to longer wavelengths (lower energies). This coupling effect is responsible for the dramatic changes in the visible color of plasmonic nanoparticle solution when nanoparticles aggregate and also the basis of many different types of plasmonic nanoparticle sensors.

### Modeling the Optical Response of Plasmonic Nanoparticles

At nanoComposix, we use modeling to predict the optical properties of a nanoparticle of a given size and shape in order to target morphologies to fabricate particles that will resonate at a desired wavelengths (e.g. with a specific laser line), as well as for quality control purposes to ensure that our nanoparticles are highly monodisperse and have uniform shapes.

Modeling for spherical nanoparticles is typically performed using Mie theory. Mie theory is not actually a theory, but rather is the analytical solution of Maxwell’s equations for the scattering of light by a spherical particle. Mie theory can be used to calculate the scattering from spheres of any diameter at any wavelength of light. While classical Mie theory assumed that the sphere would be composed of a single material, more recently, Mie theory has been adapted to solve light scattering from layered spheres. At nanoComposix, we have written a custom Mie theory code to calculate the extinction, absorption, and scattering from metal and dielectric nano- and micro-particles. Our online [Mie Theory calculator](https://nanocomposix.com/pages/tools) allows you to choose nanoparticle size, composition, and the properties of the media surrounding the particles, and calculate the scattering and absorption spectrum.

URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792

SUMMARY:
Apart from the photothermal effects and energetic charge carriers, plasmon-induced potential was proposed to be responsible for the enhancement of reaction kinetics at illuminated PMNs in newly published studies (Wilson et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020; Wilson and Jain, 2020). Alongside a growingly large number of studies on modifying chemical transformations by LSPs of PMNs, there is an urgent need for an accurate and deep mechanistic understanding of the observed plasmonic enhancement in PMCRs. It has been demonstrated that the plasmonic enhancements of PMCRs are intimately related to the confined intense electromagnetic fields, energetic charge carriers, and photothermal effects in optically excited PMNs. For example, the intense electromagnetic fields at the surface of illuminated Au NPs facilitate the polymerization of SU-8 that is a triarylium-sulfonium salt-based photo-initiator (Ueno et al., 2008).

Summary
-------

Plasmonic metal [nanostructures](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/nanomaterial "Learn more about nanostructures from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PMNs) are characterized by the [plasmon](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/plasmon "Learn more about plasmon from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") oscillation of [conduction band](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/conduction-band "Learn more about conduction band from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") electron in response to external radiation, enabling strong light absorption and scattering capacities and near-field amplification. Owing to these enhanced light-matter interactions, PMNs have garnered extensive research interest in the past decades. Notably, a growingly large number of reports show that the [energetics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/energetics "Learn more about energetics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and kinetics of chemical transformations on PMNs can be modified upon photoexcitation of their plasmons, giving rise to a new paradigm of manipulating the reaction rate and selectivity of chemical reactions. On the other hand, there is urgent need to achieve clear understanding of the mechanism underlying the photo-mediated chemical transformations on PMNs for unleashing their full potential in converting solar energy to chemicals. In this perspective, we review current fundamental concepts of photo-mediated chemical transformations executed at PMNs. Three pivotal mechanistic questions, i.e., thermal and nonthermal effects, direct and indirect charge transfer processes, and the specific impacts of plasmon-induced potentials, are explored based on recent studies. We highlight the critical aspects in which major advancements should be made to facilitate the rational design and optimization of photo-mediated chemical transformations on PMNs in the future.

Introduction
------------

Plasmonic metal [nanostructures](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/nanomaterial "Learn more about nanostructures from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PMNs) have garnered broad research interests owing to their strong interactions with incident light through localized [surface plasmons](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/surface-plasmon "Learn more about surface plasmons from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (LSPs) that are the collective oscillation of [free electrons](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/free-electron "Learn more about free electrons from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") under light excitation ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig1)A). When the frequency of electron oscillation matches that of the incident light, [localized surface plasmon resonance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/localized-surface-plasmon-resonance "Learn more about localized surface plasmon resonance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (LSPR) is established. Under the resonant conditions, PMNs display substantially larger optical extinction (absorption and scattering) cross-sections than their physical ones ([Ming et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib38)), along with remarkably intense local electric fields (|_E_|2, compared with the incident field, [Figures 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig1)B and 1C) ([Jiang et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib23)). Following the excitation ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig1)D), LSPs are relaxed by either re-emitting photons to free space (radiative decay) or yielding [energetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/energetics "Learn more about energetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") charge carriers and heating up the [crystal lattice](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/crystal-lattice "Learn more about crystal lattice from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (nonradiative decays) ([Linic et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib30)). By virtue of these unique [optical properties](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/optical-property "Learn more about optical properties from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), PMNs have been used in a wide range of applications, such as field-enhanced spectroscopy ([Willets and Van Duyne, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib69)), photodetection ([Knight et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib26)), [photovoltaics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/photovoltaics "Learn more about photovoltaics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Atwater and Polman, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib2)), imaging ([Boyer et al., 2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib9)), sensing ([Liu et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib34)), photothermal therapy ([Ding et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib17)), and so on.

![Image 12](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2589004220311792-gr1.jpg)

Figure 1. LSPR excitation and decay in PMNs, as well as their optical features

(A) Schematic illustration of collective oscillation of conduction-band electrons in PMNs.

(B) The extinction cross section versus the physical cross section of various nanoscale optical species ([Ming et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib38)).

(C) Logarithmic-scale electric field intensity amplification contours of differently shaped Au nanocrystals ([Jiang et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib23)).

(D) Radiative and nonradiative decay processes of LSPs in PMNs ([Linic et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib30)).

In recent years, [plasmon](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/plasmon "Learn more about plasmon from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") excitation of PMNs has been proved to be able to modify energetics and kinetics of chemical transformations, leading to more desirable reaction rate and/or product distribution at milder conditions than conventional thermal catalysis ([Linic et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib29), [2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib30); [Brongersma et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib10); [Zhan et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib77); [Kale et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib24); [Corson et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib15)). This burgeoning research field is commonly termed plasmon-mediated chemical reactions (PMCRs). PMCRs not only open up a new avenue to manipulate the reaction outcomes, which is otherwise difficult/unfeasible in dark conditions but also provide an efficient means for harvesting and converting solar energy through funneling the [light energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/optical-power "Learn more about light energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") into chemical bonds ([Linic et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib30); [Brongersma et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib10); [Li et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib32)). To optimize the performance of PMCRs, their underlying mechanisms must be fully understood, which is under intensive scientific studies.

PMCRs are essentially heterogeneous catalytic processes involving multiple complicated interactions of three bodies: photons, PMNs, and reaction molecules. It is generally regarded that PMNs concentrate the energy of incoming photons in small volumes through their plasmonic oscillation of [conduction electrons](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/conduction-electron "Learn more about conduction electrons from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Photothermal effects and energetic charge carriers from nonradiative plasmon decay are accounted for the observed desirable reaction results at illuminated PMNs. However, the individual contributions of photothermal effects and energetic charge carriers in PMCRs are still under vigorous debate ([Zhou et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib85), [2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib86); [Sivan et al., 2019a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib57), [2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib59); [Baffou et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib6); [Dubi et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib18); [Jain et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib91); [Sivan et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib59)). In addition, the specific mechanism by which energetic charge carriers interact with molecules that undergo chemical reactions needs to be further elucidated ([Kale et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib24); [Brongersma et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib10); [Mubeen et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib39)). Apart from the photothermal effects and energetic charge carriers, plasmon-induced potential was proposed to be responsible for the enhancement of reaction kinetics at illuminated PMNs in newly published studies ([Wilson et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib71); [Wang et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib67); [Wilson and Jain, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib70)). Herein, we give a brief introduction of pioneering and representative works about PMCRs and focus on three major aspects of the mechanism explanation, i.e., thermal and nonthermal effects, direct and indirect charge transfer, and plasmon-induced potentials. Critical viewpoints and perspectives on these issues are provided, in the hope of shedding some light on the mechanistic understanding and their industrial promises of PMCRs.

Plasmon-mediated chemical reactions
-----------------------------------

PMCRs feature a distinct reaction rate and/or product distribution at a milder reaction condition at the illuminated [PMNs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/nanomaterial "Learn more about PMNs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") than the conventional thermal reactions. In 2013, Mukherjee et al. demonstrated the enhancement of dissociation rate of H 2 at Au [nanoparticles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nanoparticle "Learn more about nanoparticles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (NPs) supported on the TiO 2 matrix under illumination ([Mukherjee et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib40)). By detecting the formation of HD molecules from the dissociations of H 2 and D 2, an almost 6-fold increase was identified ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig2)A), which can be further improved by replacing TiO 2 with SiO 2 ([Mukherjee et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib41)). Illuminated nanostructured Ag was observed to accelerate [catalytic oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/catalytic-oxidation "Learn more about catalytic oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") reactions, such as ethylene [epoxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/epoxidation "Learn more about epoxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), CO [oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxidation-reaction "Learn more about oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and NH 3 oxidation ([Christopher et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib13), [2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib14)). The steady-state reaction rate of ethylene [epoxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/epoxidation "Learn more about epoxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was increased about 4-fold with visible illumination at 250 mW/cm 2 compared with that of the thermal process ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig2)B). A recent publication revealed that excited plasmon remarkably facilitated ammonia decomposition at plasmonic antenna-reactor [photocatalyst](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/photocatalysts "Learn more about photocatalyst from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that consisted of a Cu antenna and Ru reactor sites ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig2)C) ([Zhou et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib85)).

(B) The rate of ethylene epoxidation on plasmonic Ag with/without 250 mW/cm 2 visible light illumination ([Christopher et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib13)).

(C) Real-time rate of NH 3 decomposition on plasmonic antenna-reactor photocatalyst consisting of a Cu NP antenna and Ru reactor sites with 9.6 W/cm 2 white light switched on/off ([Zhou et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib85)).

(D) Selectivity to [propylene](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/propylene "Learn more about propylene from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") epoxidation (PO) as a function of the reaction rate on Cu NPs with illumination by visible light at an intensity of 550 mW/cm 2 and in dark conditions ([Marimuthu et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib36)).

(E) Production rates of CH 4 and CO on Rh/Al 2 O 3 (left) and Au/Al 2 O 3 (right) with UV illumination at an intensity of 3W/cm 2 on/off ([Zhang et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib79)).

(F) The regioselective cleavage of unsymmetric [iodonium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/iodonium "Learn more about iodonium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") salts on plasmonic Au ([Miliutina et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib37)).

In addition to achieving a desirable reaction rate, excited [LSPs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/surface-plasmon "Learn more about LSPs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are able to manipulate product selectivity. The steady-state selectivity in [propylene](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/propene "Learn more about propylene from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") epoxidation at the surface of Cu NPs was considerably increased upon photoexcitation of Cu [LSPs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/surface-plasmon "Learn more about LSPs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig2)D) ([Marimuthu et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib36)). Excited LSPs of Al were proved effective for selective [carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/carbon-dioxide "Learn more about carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") conversion to [carbon monoxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbon-monoxide "Learn more about carbon monoxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the reverse water-gas shift reaction ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig2)E) ([Robatjazi et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib50)). Postnikov et al. even demonstrated the probability of manipulating [regioselectivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/regioselectivity "Learn more about regioselectivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in cleavage reaction of unsymmetric [iodonium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/iodonium "Learn more about iodonium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") salts by Au LSPs, opening a novel avenue for the fine control of reactivity in [organic chemistry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/organic-chemistry "Learn more about organic chemistry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#fig2)F) ([Miliutina et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib37)). Some other promising applications were reviewed in several excellent articles ([Linic et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib29), [2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib30); [Kale et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib24); [Brongersma et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib10); [Li et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib32); [Zhang et al., 2018a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib80)).

Alongside a growingly large number of studies on modifying chemical transformations by LSPs of [PMNs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nanocrystalline-material "Learn more about PMNs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), there is an urgent need for an accurate and deep mechanistic understanding of the observed plasmonic enhancement in PMCRs. It has been demonstrated that the plasmonic enhancements of PMCRs are intimately related to the confined intense [electromagnetic fields](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electromagnetic-field "Learn more about electromagnetic fields from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [energetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/energetics "Learn more about energetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") charge carriers, and photothermal effects in optically excited PMNs. For example, the intense electromagnetic fields at the surface of illuminated Au NPs facilitate the polymerization of SU-8 that is a triarylium-sulfonium salt-based photo-initiator ([Ueno et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib63)). The photothermal effects promote the decomposition of dicumyl [peroxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/peroxide "Learn more about peroxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to 2-phenyl-2-propanol and [acetophenone](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/acetophenone "Learn more about acetophenone from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on illuminated Au NPs ([Fasciani et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib20)). Energetic charge carriers enhance [carbon dioxide reduction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/carbon-dioxide-reduction "Learn more about carbon dioxide reduction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), O 2 dissociation and oxidation reactions, H 2 dissociation and hydrogenation reactions, N 2 fixation and NH 3 decomposition, and CO 2 reduction and/or dissociation on PMNs ([Li et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib32)). More encouragingly, some studies at single particle and single turnover event level provide invaluable insights into the fundamental processes of PMCRs, e.g., the rate-determining step and its [activation energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/reaction-activation-energy "Learn more about activation energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and the spatial distribution of reaction sites ([Zhao and Chen, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib82); [Tachikawa et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib61)). By using single-molecule fluorescence, the plasmon enhanced-fluorogenic oxidation of Amplex Red by H 2 O 2 on a single Au [nanorod](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nanorod "Learn more about nanorod from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was investigated with sub-turnover resolution ([Li et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589004220311792#bib33)). The activation energies of intermediate generation, product generation, and product desorption were clearly differentiated, showing that the excited LSPs mainly accelerate the intermediate generation (rate-limiting step) by substantially lowering its [activation energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/activation-energy "Learn more about activation energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Dionne et al. demonstrated that LSPs drive reaction nucleation at specific sites closest to the electromagnetic hot spots in the [dehydrogenation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/dehydrogenation "Learn more about dehydrogenation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of Pd nanocubes adjacent to an Au [nanodisc](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/nanodisc "Learn more about nanodisc from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Page

Abstract
--------

In the past half-century, surface plasmon resonance in noble metallic nanoparticles has been an important research subject. Recent advances in the synthesis, assembly, characterization, and theories of traditional and non-traditional metal nanostructures open a new pathway to the kaleidoscopic applications of plasmonics. However, accurate and precise models of plasmon resonance are still challenging, as its characteristics can be affected by multiple factors. We herein summarize the recent advances of plasmonic nanoparticles and their applications, particularly regarding the fundamentals and applications of surface plasmon resonance (SPR) in Au nanoparticles, plasmon-enhanced upconversion luminescence, and plasmonic chiral metasurfaces.

**Keywords:** plasmonic nanoparticles, upconversion luminescence, chiral metasurfaces

1. Introduction
---------------

Surface plasmon resonance (SPR) is the interaction between electromagnetic fields and free electrons in metals. The free electrons in metals can be excited by electrical components of light to produce collective oscillations. There are two modes formed by such electron oscillations. One is the surface plasmon–polariton (SPP), which can propagate along the metal/dielectric interfaces. The other one is the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR), which is confined in a very small volume around an isolated nanoparticle/nanostructure. In both cases, the incident electromagnetic field can be localized at a deep subwavelength scale with a remarkable enhancement of the local field, leading to a broad range of applications in the fields of biology, chemistry, energy, and information [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B1-materials-11-01833),[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B2-materials-11-01833),[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B3-materials-11-01833),[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B4-materials-11-01833),[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B5-materials-11-01833),[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B6-materials-11-01833),[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B7-materials-11-01833)]. Typical applications in bioimaging [[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B8-materials-11-01833)], sensing [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B2-materials-11-01833),[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B9-materials-11-01833),[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B10-materials-11-01833)], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B11-materials-11-01833),[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B12-materials-11-01833),[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B13-materials-11-01833),[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B14-materials-11-01833)], chemical reactions [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B15-materials-11-01833)], and metasurface [[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B16-materials-11-01833)] have been reported based on the unique characteristics of plasmonics. The significant influence of plasmonics on nanoscience and nanotechnology lies in three major points. (1) Plasmons can resonate with light at a particular frequency to a great degree of freedom; for example, 0D metallic nanoparticles (NPs) and nanorods (NRs) [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B2-materials-11-01833),[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B17-materials-11-01833)], one-dimensional (1D) nanowires [[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B18-materials-11-01833)], and two-dimensional (2D) metasurfaces can be used to highly control/utilize LSPR to achieve the selective absorption or reflection of light. (2) Plasmons can produce an enormous enhancement of the light intensity [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B11-materials-11-01833)]. (3) Plasmons can trap electromagnetic energy in deep subwavelength volumes/areas/lengths [[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B19-materials-11-01833),[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B20-materials-11-01833),[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B21-materials-11-01833)].

Early recognition of LSPR came from the selective absorption of metallic NPs in the visible light range [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B2-materials-11-01833)]. The theoretical description can be well treated by the free electron gas model. Thus far, theoretical advances of plasmonics have led to the great development of design for plasmonic materials and applications of various plasmonic devices. For example, the plasmon effect based on noble metal NPs is applied to refractive index sensing, SERS, and the enhancement of solar energy conversion [[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B5-materials-11-01833)]. Through artificially designing the plasmon resonance with the specific geometries and frequencies, metal NPs can be also used to modulate the luminescence of the emitting nanomaterials. A typical example is to utilize LSPR to enhance the luminescence of the upconversion materials. With the advanced development of nanotechnology, the production and performance of noble metal nanodevices have been rising to an unprecedented height. In addition, the advances in nanoscience and nanotechnology have provided a new platform to study the interaction of plasmonic nanostructures with circularly polarized light for spin–orbit photonics [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B2-materials-11-01833)]. In this review, we summarize the recent research progress and achievements on LSPR, particularly including: (1) fundamentals and applications of LSPR in Au NPs, (2) plasmon enhanced upconversion luminescence, and (3) plasmonic chiral metasurfaces.

2. Fundamentals and Applications of Plasmonics in Au NPs
--------------------------------------------------------

### 2.1. Fundamentals

In order to understand the physical mechanism of surface plasmons, it is necessary to enlighten the model of the resonant oscillation of the free electrons in metals ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#materials-11-01833-f001)). Under the excitation of an external electric field, the motion of free electrons in metal NPs can be described by the following equation [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B1-materials-11-01833)]:

m d 2 x d t 2+m γ d x d t=−e E 0 exp(−i ε t),(1)

where _m_, _γ_, _ω_, and _E_ 0 are the electron mass, damping constant, frequency, and amplitude of the external electric field, respectively. To include the size effect on the optical permittivity of metals, a well-known Drude–Lorentz model has been successfully developed. For the isolated metallic NPs with the size comparable to the incident wavelength, the generated electrons inside the NPs are considered as an ideal gas of non-interacting charged particles under Newton’s second law. The frequency-dependent Drude permittivity of metal NPs can be written as:

ε(ω)=ε r+i ε i=1−ω p 2 ω(ω+i γ),(2)

where _ω_ p is the plasmon frequency, which is close ~10 16 s−1. When γ<<_ω_, Equation (2) becomes _ε_(_ω_) _≈_ 1 − _ω_ p 2/_ω_ 2. Thus, the dielectric constant _ε_(_ω_) can be negative once _ω_<_ω_ p, and there is a strong interaction between metal and incident electromagnetic waves. In contrast, when _ω_>_ω_ p, metal is just a conventional dielectric material for an incident electromagnetic field. For the case of metallic NPs with a size far less than the incidence wavelength, the interband transitions will play a major role. Equation (2) is a subset condition of the Drude–Lorentz model, which can be used to explain the physical mechanism of surface plasmons. The Drude–Lorentz model can work well for the case of isolated NPs. There are also other approaches that can provide similar results to Equation (2). For example, the permittivity of isolated metal NP suspended in a medium has been well described by Maxwell–Garnett approach. The permittivity of the connected NPs has also been explained by Bruggeman approach. However, when the NPs are adjacent to each other or in an array arrangement, the electric fields generated from NPs will be coupled each other. Depending on the distance between the NPs, geometry, and light polarization, plasmonic resonances of plasmonic devices (or structures) show the hybrid modes. Typical examples are the Fano resonances and the LSPR splitting ([Figure 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#materials-11-01833-f002)). In the following, we will focus on the LSPR nature of noble metal NPs and LSRP corresponding applications.

The various applications of plasmonic NPs are associated with two important physical effects. One is that the optical extinction of the metallic NPs has a maximum at visible near-infrared (NIR) wavelengths, which is much larger than their geometrical size. For example, Au NPs that are larger than 2 nm have approximatively 100% light-to-heat conversion efficiency, which is caused by their large extinction cross-section [[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B22-materials-11-01833)]. The heated NPs have many potential applications, such as the photothermal therapy in treating cancers [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B4-materials-11-01833)]. The other is the localized enhancement of the electromagnetic field near the particles. In case the metallic spherical NPs have a size comparable to the metal skin depth (~100 nm), the electron can be driven by the light wave with a resonant frequency. As shown in [Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#materials-11-01833-f001)a, the electron gas oscillates similar to a dipole in the parallel direction to the electric field. Since the resulting plasmonic oscillation is distributed over the whole particle volume, the NPs act similar to a nanoantenna [[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B23-materials-11-01833)].

The optical properties of LSPR in an NP system, such as quality factor, resonance bandwidth, local field intensity, and so on, are influenced by many external and internal factors. For example, in a metallic NP dispersion, the measured optical extinction spectrum is the sum of absorbed and scattered photons. We can mathematically treat the extinction spectrum of NPs as the sum of the absorption and scattering terms:

σ E x t=σ A b s+σ S c a(3)

The absorption and scattering of NPs are strongly dependent on the particle size. When the metallic NPs are much smaller than the wavelength of light ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#materials-11-01833-f001)c), the absorption becomes much stronger than the scattering. When the diameter of the NP is comparable to the incident wavelength, multipolar plasmon oscillation occurs inside the NP. As shown in [Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#materials-11-01833-f001)d, both absorption and scattering become comparable in this case. Thus, the size of NPs leads to the change of the optical constants of NPs [[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B8-materials-11-01833)].

The LSPR intensity can be quantified by the quality factor. Following Equation (2), the quality factor of plasmonic NPs, _Q_ NP, can be written as [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B1-materials-11-01833)]:

Q NP(ω)=−R e[ε(ω)]I m[ε(ω)],(4)

For a given resonance frequency _ω_, the resonance quality factor of a gold nanosphere depends only on the real and imaginary parts of its dielectric constant. A higher _Q_ NP factor corresponds to a spectrally narrowband and stronger local field enhancement. When the mean free path of conduction electrons is comparable to the particle size, the scattering by the free electron at the particle surface is immeasurable. Specifically, when the particle size is smaller than 30 nm, _Q_ NP decreased with the NP size. On the other hand, the permittivity of LSPR system can be controlled by the dielectric environment around the NPs. Following Equation (4), the change of _ε_(_ω_) leads to variation of the resonance frequency and _Q_ NP [[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B8-materials-11-01833)]. The polarizability dependence of the Au NPs on the frequency is shown in [Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#materials-11-01833-f001)b. The resonance frequency is redshifted with the increased _Re_[_ε_(_ω_)], and the NP extinction cross-section is increased at the same time. For the absorbing dielectric environment, the geometry of the absorbing materials and the coupling distance play a particularly prominent role. The imaginary part of the permittivity will weaken or even eliminate the LSPR resonance [[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B23-materials-11-01833)].

When the gold NPs are close to each other, the plasmon resonances are coupled to produce a variety of optical modes ([Figure 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#materials-11-01833-f002)a). For specific NP assembly geometries, which are typically associated with well-defined NP disposition and homogeneous nanometric gaps between particles, the mutual interaction between plasmon oscillations can originate constructive and destructive interference phenomena yielding to asymmetric LSPR band shapes, and sharp dips or spikes in the optical extinction spectrum, which are known as Fano resonances [[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B24-materials-11-01833)]. It has been reported that the strong coupling can be achieved by the Au NP on the mirror system, which has a ~1 nm gap between the NP and the metallic mirror [[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B25-materials-11-01833)]. [Figure 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#materials-11-01833-f002)b–d show the strong coupling of a single molecule in plasmonic nanocavities at room temperature. Since the plasmonic resonance mode volume is less than 40 nm 3, remarkable energy-level splitting is achieved in ordinary ambient conditions where there is no low temperature and no high-Q optical cavity. In addition, non-ambient conditions such as low temperature, high pressure, etc., can also have significant impact on the characteristics of the LSPR [[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B26-materials-11-01833),[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B27-materials-11-01833)].

### 2.2. Applications of SPR in Au NPs

When the free electrons in the NPs resonate with the incident electromagnetic field, a strongly localized and enhanced electric field is created in the subwavelength regime. As a result, the NP acts as a nanolens to focus the electromagnetic field. This principle provides a method to enhance several types of optical phenomena in the nearby molecules or nanomaterials, such as Raman scattering [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B11-materials-11-01833),[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B28-materials-11-01833)], photoluminescence, extinction, and nonlinear effects [[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B29-materials-11-01833)]. The enhancement factor near the metallic NP surface can be expressed as _EF = E_ loc/_E_ 0, where the _E_ loc and _E_ 0 are the local and incident electric field, respectively. The _EF_ can reach 10 2–10 3 in most LSPR cases by using the metallic NPs [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B11-materials-11-01833)]. For similarly sized nanospheres and nanotetrahedrons, the _EF_ of nanospheres is smaller than the nanotetrahedrons because the nanotetrahedrons have sharper edges or tips, which allow free electrons to localize at the tip to produce a much more intense field [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B2-materials-11-01833)]. In case of an NP dimer or trimer, the largest _EF_ is possible as the junctions occur between NPs [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B4-materials-11-01833)].

#### 2.2.1. Surface Enhancement Raman Scattering

The far-field radiation by LSP is well-employed in the surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS). [Figure 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#materials-11-01833-f003)a displays a schematic of SERS by Au NPs. If the LSPR occurs beside the Raman scattering molecules, the low efficiency of Raman scattering can be strongly improved, which represents the structure effect of the plasmonics ([Figure 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#materials-11-01833-f003)b). The total SERS enhancement factor _E_ SERS depends on two terms as _E_ SERS = _E_ em _E_ chem, where _E_ em and _E_ chem account for the increase of the surface Raman signal due to the electromagnetic effect and the chemical effect, respectively [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B11-materials-11-01833),[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6213938/#B30-materials-11-01833)]. Commonly, the _E_ em term can reach values as high as ~10 10 inside a hot spot with the smallest mode resonance volume. However, the average _E_ em over the whole Au NP surface leads to the reduced enhancement. Experimentally measured SERS results show that the averaging _E_ em can reach 10 7 by an Au NP substrate. _E_ chem is normally less than 10 2. The chemical enhancement is formatted by the charge-transfer state between the NP surface and the molecule, which can be tuned by the excitation laser.

Abstract
--------

Plasmonics is a quickly developing subject that combines fundamental research and applications ranging from areas such as physics to engineering, chemistry, biology, medicine, food sciences, and the environmental sciences. Plasmonics appeared in the 1950s with the discovery of surface plasmon polaritons. Then, plasmonics went through a novel impulsion in mid-1970s when the surface-enhanced Raman scattering was discovered. Nevertheless, it is in this last decade that a very significant explosion of plasmonics and its applications has occurred. Thus, this special issue reports a snapshot of current advances in these various areas of plasmonics and its applications presented in the format of several articles and reviews written by worldwide researchers of this topic.

**Keywords:** plasmonics, sensing, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, sum-frequency generation, third harmonic generation, surface-enhanced fluorescence, metasurfaces, catalysis, lanthanum hexaboride, solar cell

1. Introduction
---------------

Plasmonics (or nanoplasmonics) is a young topic of research, which is part of nanophotonics and nano-optics. Plasmonics concerns to the investigation of electron oscillations in metallic nanostructures and nanoparticles (NPs). Surface plasmons have optical properties, which are very interesting. For instance, surface plasmons have the unique capacity to confine light at the nanoscale [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B1-materials-12-01502),[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B2-materials-12-01502),[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B3-materials-12-01502)]. Moreover, surface plasmons are very sensitive to the surrounding medium and the properties of the materials on which they propagate. In addition to the above, the surface plasmon resonances can be controlled by adjusting the size, shape, periodicity, and materials nature. Indeed, the technological progress allows researchers to produce new plasmonic systems by controlling all the parameters described previously [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B4-materials-12-01502),[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B5-materials-12-01502),[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B6-materials-12-01502),[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B7-materials-12-01502),[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B8-materials-12-01502),[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B9-materials-12-01502),[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B10-materials-12-01502),[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B11-materials-12-01502),[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B12-materials-12-01502),[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B13-materials-12-01502),[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B14-materials-12-01502)]. Moreover, theoretical, computational, and numerical simulation tools have been developed in this last decade, allowing for a better understanding of the optical properties of plasmonic systems [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B1-materials-12-01502)]. Thus, all these optical properties of plasmonic systems can enable a great number of applications, such as biosensors [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B15-materials-12-01502),[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B16-materials-12-01502),[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B17-materials-12-01502),[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B18-materials-12-01502),[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B19-materials-12-01502),[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B20-materials-12-01502)], optical devices [[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B21-materials-12-01502),[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B22-materials-12-01502),[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B23-materials-12-01502),[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B24-materials-12-01502)], and photovoltaic devices [[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B25-materials-12-01502),[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B26-materials-12-01502),[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B27-materials-12-01502),[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B28-materials-12-01502)].

2. Synopsis
-----------

This special issue is composed of five review articles, five research articles, and two communications. The first part of the latter is devoted to the applications of plasmonics to physics and engineering [[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B29-materials-12-01502),[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B30-materials-12-01502),[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B31-materials-12-01502),[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B32-materials-12-01502),[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B33-materials-12-01502)]. Concerning the applications to physics, such as non-linear optics, Mattox et al. demonstrated the control of plasmonic properties of LaB 6 via Eu-doping on a spectral range from near-infrared to infrared [[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B29-materials-12-01502)]. Then, Mattox et al. presented a review on the tuning of the plasmonic resonance of lanthanum hexaboride for a potential application to solar heat absorption [[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B30-materials-12-01502)]. Besides, Ogata et al. investigated the effect of the plasmonic resonance of metallic nanostructures on the optical third harmonic generation (THG) enhancement of nickel nanostructure-covered microcubes [[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B31-materials-12-01502)]. For the application to photovoltaics, Hajjiah et al. presented a simulation study of the efficiency enhancement of peroskite solar cells by using plasmonic nanoparticles [[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B32-materials-12-01502)]. To finish this first part dedicated to physics with the application to metasurfaces, Li et al. proposed a novel computational method in order to optimize the coupling of the electric fields of a metasurface consisting of nanorod plasmonic antennas. This novel computational method is based on the coupling of the decomposition into several orders [[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B33-materials-12-01502)].

In the second and last part, the discussed topics are devoted to chemistry and sensing, such as surface-enhanced fluorescence, surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), sum-frequency generation (SFG) spectroscopy, and electrocatalysis by using plasmonics [[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B34-materials-12-01502),[35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B35-materials-12-01502),[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B36-materials-12-01502),[37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B37-materials-12-01502),[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B38-materials-12-01502),[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B39-materials-12-01502),[40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B40-materials-12-01502)]. Concerning the surface-enhanced fluorescence, Lu et al. numerically demonstrated a high enhancement effect of the fluorescence signal obtained with a hybrid metal-dielectric nano-aperture antenna consisting of silicon and gold layers [[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B34-materials-12-01502)]. Besides, for the SERS topic, Magno et al. showed excellent analytical enhancement factors of the SERS signal obtained with hybrid Al/Si nanopillars for the detection of thiophenol molecules. These hybrid Al/Si nanopillars have been realized with a simple and quick fabrication technique [[35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B35-materials-12-01502)]. Moreover, Sarychev et al. presented a review on the light concentration by metal-dielectric micro/nano-resonators for efficient SERS sensing. In this review, the recent advances in this topic of metal-dielectric micro/nano-resonators for SERS are exposed [[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B36-materials-12-01502)]. Furthermore, D’Orlando et al. showed the feasibility to carry out and control nanostructures of gold nanoparticles, which can be seen as plasmonic molecules whose optical resonances are tuned by modifying the shape, symmetry, and interparticle distances with an AFM (Atomic Force Microscope) device coupled with an optical spectrometer [[37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B37-materials-12-01502)]. To complete the sensing part, Han et al. presented a short review on plasmonic biosensing based on the design of nanovoids in thin films by reviewing resonance modes, materials, and hybrid functions using simultaneously electrical conductivity [[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B38-materials-12-01502)]. In addition, Humbert et al. presented a review on the sum-frequency generation (SFG) spectroscopy of plasmonic nanomaterials. In this review, the authors introduced the fundamentals of SFG spectroscopy. Then, they presented an overview of studies of plasmonic nanomaterials by this SFG spectroscopy over the last five years [[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B39-materials-12-01502)]. To conclude this part, as well as this special issue dedicated to “_Plasmonics and its Applications_”, Subramanian et al. presented a review on the electrocatalysis induced by plasmon with multi-component nanostructures. Indeed, the authors highlight the recent progress obtained in the synthesis of these multi-component nanostructures, especially for the plasmonic electrocatalysis of major fuel-forming and fuel cell reactions [[40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6539533/#B40-materials-12-01502)].

3. Conclusions
--------------

In making this special issue on plasmonics and its applications, I had the pleasure of obtaining contributions from high-quality authors worldwide, and I thank them for that. To conclude, I hope that this special issue dedicated to plasmonics and its applications will be read with interest by the students or researchers who wish to be involved in this topic or to gain an advanced understanding of it.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Plasmonic nanostructures are crucial for advancing fields like chemical sensing, biological imaging, and energy harvesting due to their unique light-matter interaction capabilities.
Surface plasmons arise from the collective oscillation of conduction electrons in metals, enhancing light absorption and scattering, which is pivotal for applications like SERS.
Various methods exist for creating plasmonic nanostructures, including self-assembly and templated synthesis, allowing precise control over their optical properties and dimensions.
Applications of plasmonic nanostructures range from enhancing spectroscopic techniques to enabling novel energy conversion methods, demonstrating their versatility across numerous scientific fields.
